food_subgroup: Fish curry / South Indian seafood curry

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'fish curry' class; no Tamil-specific Meen Kulambu entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a spiced fish curry preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Meen Kulambu. A0FJP (cooked fish) is the closest defensible anchor; tamarind-spice curry preparation is not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Meen Kulambu or Tamil fish curry as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Meen Kulambu is approximately 250 g, representing one bowl of fish curry typically consumed alongside steamed rice or parboiled rice at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides nutrient data for Indian fish preparations and individual spice ingredients. Values for Meen Kulambu are adapted from fish curry and cooked fish entries in ICMR-NIN, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for individual ingredients (tamarind, shallots, spices). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central fish and oil data. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not directly reported for this composite preparation and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — peer-reviewed analogous estimate for high-protein, tamarind-acidified fish curry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Meen Kulambu. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous high-protein fish curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 International GI Tables. The tamarind-rich acidic base, high protein content (13.8 g/100 g), and minimal starch content (4.5 g carbohydrate/100 g) are consistent with a low GI profile. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (35 × 11.25) / 100 ≈ 3.9, rounded to 4.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 380 mg/100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, representing ~41% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and reduce added salt during preparation, as the sodium from tamarind, salt, and preserved spice pastes can accumulate significantly; pairing with potassium-rich foods such as leafy greens may help offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and high protein (13.8 g/100 g) with minimal carbohydrate (4.5 g/100 g) result in a low glycemic load of approximately 4 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Meen Kulambu is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions (250 g) alongside moderate amounts of parboiled or basmati rice, as the high protein and acidic tamarind base slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fish provides omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA); garlic, shallots, and black pepper contribute cardioprotective phytochemicals; saturated fat is low at approximately 1.3 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of Meen Kulambu prepared with oily fish such as kingfish or mackerel can support cardiovascular health through omega-3 intake; however, sodium should be controlled by reducing added salt and using fresh rather than preserved tamarind to keep sodium below 600 mg per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids from fish; curcumin-adjacent spices (turmeric if used) and garlic reduce systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Meen Kulambu is generally beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its fish-derived omega-3 content and anti-inflammatory spice profile; no specific restrictions apply unless the individual has a fish allergy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Meen Kulambu is a foundational Tamil fish curry from Tamil Nadu, South India, distinguished by its intensely sour, spicy, and aromatic gravy built on a generous base of tamarind pulp. Fresh fish — commonly tilapia, kingfish (seer fish), or anchovies — is slow-simmered in a sauce of sautéed shallots, garlic, and tomatoes, seasoned with red chili powder, coriander powder, and black pepper. The dish is finished with a tempering (tadka) of fenugreek seeds and mustard seeds in hot oil, which imparts a characteristic earthy bitterness. The tamarind acts as both a souring agent and a natural preservative, and the dish is widely noted to improve in depth and complexity 12–24 hours after cooking. Nutritionally, Meen Kulambu is high in protein (13.8 g/100 g), moderate in fat (5.2 g/100 g), and low in carbohydrates (4.5 g/100 g), yielding approximately 115 kcal per 100 g. It is a meaningful source of vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg/100 g), niacin (3.2 mg/100 g), selenium (22.5 mcg/100 g), and phosphorus (175 mg/100 g). Its low glycemic index (~35) and high protein content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content (380 mg/100 g) warrants attention for those with hypertension. The dish is gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal, and is typically served at lunch or dinner alongside steamed or parboiled rice.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for individual ingredients. No single approved-source entry exists for Meen Kulambu as a composite dish; values represent a well-supported adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Saturated fat is estimated from fish and oil composition data. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water are not directly reported for this preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Meen Kulambu as a composite Tamil fish curry preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. Saturated fat (1.3 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked fish (tilapia/kingfish) and cooking oil contribution. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Meen Kulambu was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. Portions in the input JSON contained four identical duplicate entries (250 g each); deduplicated to a single 250 g serving. The input listed 'diabetes' as a health condition key; this has been mapped to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. Vitamin A value (15.0 mcg/100 g) is expressed as mcg RAE. Folate (18.0 mcg/100 g) is expressed as mcg DFE.
